title: APPENDIX — Quick-reference summary (not for reading aloud)

lines.text: APPENDIX — Quick-reference summary (not for reading aloud)
Core idea: retroviruses break the central dogma — RNA genome reverse-transcribed to DNA by virion-packaged reverse transcriptase (RT), then integrated into host chromosome as a permanent provirus.
Particle: ~100 nm, enveloped, with Env (SU + TM); matrix MA inside membrane; conical capsid CA (HIV) enclosing diploid (+)ssRNA genome bound by NC, plus RT, IN, PR enzymes.
Genome: 5' cap—R—U5—PBS—gag—pol—env—PPT—U3—R—poly-A.
LTRs (U3-R-U5) are generated during RT, not pre-existing.

Life cycle: entry (receptor + coreceptor + fusion) → RT (two strand transfers, LTRs built) → integration (PIC, integrase strand transfer) → transcription (host RNA pol II from 5' LTR; Tat–TAR releases pause) → splicing/export (Rev–RRE–CRM1 for incompletely spliced RNAs) → translation (Gag, Gag-Pol via −1 frameshift, ~20:1 ratio; Env via secretory pathway) → assembly at PM (Gag oligomerizes, NC grabs psi-marked RNA) → budding (ESCRT via p6 late domains) → maturation (PR cleaves Gag/Gag-Pol
conical capsid → infectious).
HIV-specific: CD4 + CCR5 or CXCR4; gp120/gp41; CCR5-Δ32 homozygotes resistant to R5 virus; lentivirus crosses intact nuclear envelope (so transduces non-dividing cells).
Drug classes by life-cycle step: Entry: enfuvirtide (gp41 fusion), maraviroc (CCR5), fostemsavir (CD4 attachment), ibalizumab (anti-CD4 mAb).
RT: NRTIs (chain terminators — AZT, tenofovir, lamivudine, emtricitabine, abacavir); NNRTIs (allosteric — efavirenz, rilpivirine, doravirine).
Integration: INSTIs (raltegravir, dolutegravir, bictegravir, cabotegravir).
Maturation: PIs (darunavir, atazanavir, usually boosted with ritonavir/cobicistat).
Capsid: lenacapavir (long-acting).
Resistance: RT error ~10^-4–10^-5/base × ~10^10 virions/day × no proofreading → every mutation generated daily → quasispecies → monotherapy fails.
Combination of 3 drugs from ≥2 classes is the foundation of cART.
Signature mutations: M184V (3TC/FTC), K103N (1st-gen NNRTI), K65R (TDF), Q151M / T215Y (older NRTIs), N155H / Q148K (INSTI).
ERVs: ~8% of human genome.
Syncytin-1 (HERV-W env) essential for placental syncytiotrophoblast formation — co-opted retroviral fusogen.
Vectors: SIN (self-inactivating, U3-deleted) lentiviral vectors with VSV-G envelope, third-generation split-packaging.
Lentivirus transduces non-dividing cells.
Insertional mutagenesis risk (gammaretroviral LMO2 → leukemia in X-SCID trials) drove SIN design.
Clinical: Strimvelis (ADA-SCID), Zynteglo (β-thalassemia), Skysona (ALD), all CAR-T (Kymriah, Yescarta, Tecartus, Breyanzi, Abecma, Carvykti).
Oncoretroviruses: v-src in RSV → c-src in chicken genome → cellular proto-oncogene concept (Varmus/Bishop, Nobel 1989).
Human: HTLV-1 → adult T-cell leukemia/lymphoma and HAM/TSP, via Tax and HBZ regulatory proteins.

Cure strategies: shock-and-kill (LRAs: HDACi, BET inhibitors, PKC agonists), block-and-lock (Tat inhibitors like dCA), CCR5 gene editing (ZFN, CRISPR), CCR5-Δ32 allogeneic stem cell transplant (Berlin, London, Düsseldorf patients), broadly neutralizing antibodies (VRC01, 3BNC117, 10-1074).
Reservoir is the central obstacle: long-lived latently infected resting memory CD4 T cells with integrated, silent, replication-competent provirus.

Two organizing principles: (1) every step of the retroviral life cycle is a drug target — anatomy of the cycle predicts the pharmacology; (2) the line between virus and host is porous — integration is permanent, ERVs are abundant, co-option happens, and gene therapy weaponizes that biology for medicine.
